title: Individually tunable Si/SiGe quantum dot operating voltages via gate-biased illumination
authors: Jared Benson, Sanghyeok Park, Owen M. Eskandari, M. A. Wolfe, Brighton X. Coe, J. P. Dodson, S. N. Coppersmith, Mark Friesen, M. A. Eriksson
abstract: Semiconductor quantum dot qubits often require very different voltages on each gate to bring them to a correct operating point. Here, we present a method by which one can controllably and repeatably alter the nanoscale trapped charge distribution at an oxide-semiconductor interface. We demonstrate this method on a Si/SiGe quantum dot device, and we find that the operating voltages can be controlled and made much more uniform. The method relies on illumination with near-infrared light in the presence of applied gate voltages, and it enables the tuning of the device operating point on a gate-by-gate basis. We present an explanation of the underlying physics using self-consistent Schrödinger-Poisson simulations. As an application of this method, we tune a triple quantum dot to have uniform and small operating voltages in the (1,1,1) charge configuration. Importantly, we show that shifting the operating voltages in this way does not change the measured charge noise.
summary: The authors demonstrate a technique called gate-biased illumination to precisely and repeatably tune the operating voltages of Si/SiGe quantum dot qubits. By trapping charges at the oxide interface using near-infrared light, they can achieve uniform, low-voltage operation across multiple gates without detrimentally affecting the measured charge noise, advancing the scalability of solid-state quantum computing.
main_problem: Semiconductor quantum dot qubits require significantly different gate voltages on each gate to reach the correct operating point, complicating device tuning and limiting low-power operation.
main_result: Gate-biased illumination allows for the controllable and repeatable tuning of operating voltages on a gate-by-gate basis, enabling uniform and small operating voltages without increasing charge noise.
method: The technique involves illuminating the Si/SiGe quantum dot device with near-infrared light while applying specific DC gate voltages, which modifies the trapped charge distribution at the oxide-semiconductor interface.
model_or_system: The platform is a Si/SiGe quantum dot device patterned on a heterostructure. The mechanism relies on photo-generated electron-hole pairs trapping charges at the oxide-semiconductor interface, which shifts the local electric fields and thus the operating voltages.
key_observables: Operating voltages, pinch-off voltages ($V_{PO}$), and charge noise ($S_{1/2}^{\epsilon}$ at 1 Hz).
important_parameters: Near-infrared illumination (e.g., 780 nm laser), gate voltages, and the resulting voltage shifts (e.g., reducing voltage difference from 340 mV to 114 mV).
main_assumptions: The trapped charge distribution established during illumination remains 'frozen' in place, and the charge noise measurement is unaffected by this voltage shift.
figures_summary: Figures show the device structure, stability diagrams illustrating low-voltage operation after illumination, and comparisons of charge noise spectra before and after tuning.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-uniform gate voltages, details the gate-biased illumination method, presents experimental measurements of voltage shifts and charge noise, and validates the underlying physics using self-consistent Schrödinger-Poisson simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a novel, electrically controllable method to engineer the electrostatic environment of solid-state qubits, which is crucial for scaling up quantum processors and minimizing power consumption.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Gate-biased illumination enables low-voltage operation of quantum dots. a A false-color SEM micrograph of a device that is nominally identical to the one measured here. b-d Cross-sections of the gate stack and upper layers of the Si/Si0.7Ge0.3 heterostructure along the white dashed line in (a) showing the gate-biased illumination process. b Large and non-uniform gate voltages are required to form a triple quantum dot due to fixed oxide charges and interface trapped charges (not shown). c The device is illuminated with a 780 nm laser while its gates are biased with dc voltages. Photo-generated electron-hole pairs (white-black ovals) move to screen the electric fields in the quantum...

Figure 2: Characterizing gate-biased illumination by simulating and measuring gate pinch-off voltage shifts. a Experimental pinch-off curves for the P2 plunger gate measured before and after illuminating the device with VP2 = −0.5 V. The pinch-off voltages from each measurement are indicated by the vertical dashed lines and the pinch-off voltage shift is the difference between the initial and final pinch-off voltages. The voltages applied to the gates during this measurement are shown in the inset of (b). b The model used to solve the charge density at the oxide- semiconductor interface during the gate-biased illumination. Here, accumulation occurs in charge-trap states at the interface...

Figure 3: Experimental (diamonds) and simulated (circles) shifts in pinch-off voltages showing good agreement for the following cases of gate-biased illumination: a case 1, large voltage difference between plunger and barrier gates (−0.5 V P1-P3 and 0 V B1-B4), b case 2, smaller difference in plunger and barrier gate voltages (−0.2 V P1-P3 and −0.1 V B1-B4). For the experimental results, the data points are the mean pinch-off voltage shifts from 11 repetitions of each illumination and the error bars are the standard deviations. The green lines indicate the voltages applied to each gate during the illumination.

Figure 4: Charge noise comparison between a typical operating regime and a low-voltage operating regime. Main panel: charge noise S1/2 ε at 1 Hz for each of the first 29 charge sensor Coulomb peaks at a typical device operation point compared to the same peaks in a low-voltage operating regime following a gate-biased illumination. Inset panels: the Coulomb peaks used when measuring the charge noise in each case. More details about the charge noise measurements can be found in the Supporting Information.
